{{Short description="Device that emits coherent light through stimulated emission}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Laser
| image = Laser pointers.jpg
| caption = Laser beams from various laser pointers.
| topics = [[Optics]], [[Photon]], [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Electromagnetic radiation]]
| acronym = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
}}

A '''laser''' is a device that emits [[light]] through a process of optical amplification based on the [[stimulated emission]] of [[electromagnetic radiation]]. The word "laser" is an acronym for "Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of [[Radiation]]".

Lasers differ from other light sources in that they emit light that is:
* '''[[Coherence (physics)|Coherent]]''' — all photons are in phase
* '''Monochromatic''' — single wavelength (colour)
* '''Directional''' — narrow beam with low divergence

== Principle of operation ==
A laser consists of three essential components:

=== 1. Gain medium ===
A material (gas, liquid, solid, or semiconductor) with atoms or molecules that can be excited to a higher [[energy level]].

=== 2. Pumping mechanism ===
Energy source that excites atoms to create a [[population inversion]] — more atoms in the upper excited state than the lower state. Methods:
* Optical pumping (flash lamps, other lasers)
* Electrical discharge
* Chemical reactions

=== 3. Optical resonator ===
A pair of [[mirror]]s forming a [[Fabry–Pérot cavity]] that provides optical feedback. Photons reflect back and forth, stimulating further emissions and amplifying the beam.

== Stimulated emission ==
The key quantum process: an incoming [[photon]] of energy <math>h\nu = E_2 - E_1</math> stimulates an excited atom to emit an identical photon — same frequency, phase, direction, and polarization.

== Laser types ==
* '''Gas lasers''' — [[helium–neon laser|He–Ne]], CO₂, argon-ion
* '''Solid-state lasers''' — Nd:YAG, [[ruby laser|ruby]], titanium:sapphire
* '''Semiconductor lasers''' — laser diodes (CD/DVD players, fibre optics)
* '''Dye lasers''' — tunable wavelength
* '''Fiber lasers''' — high-power industrial applications
* '''Free-electron lasers''' — tunable across wide spectrum

== Applications ==
* Telecommunications — [[optical fibre]] data transmission
* Medicine — [[laser surgery]], eye correction ([[LASIK]])
* Manufacturing — cutting, welding, engraving
* Scientific research — [[spectroscopy]], [[laser cooling]], [[optical tweezers]]
* Consumer electronics — barcode scanners, printers, disc readers
* Military — rangefinding, target designation
* Metrology — precise distance measurement ([[LIDAR]])

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Coherence (physics)]]
* [[Optics]]
* [[Photon]]
* [[Spectroscopy]]
* [[Stimulated emission]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Laser| ]]
[[Category:Optics]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
